title: The Rise of DIY Apps: How Start-ups Are Turning Novices Into Developers
description: Start-ups like Kineto are using AI to make app creation accessible to everyone, challenging traditional software development models.
lede: The era of the specialized coder might be facing new competition. A fresh wave of start-ups is leveraging artificial intelligence to empower absolute beginners to build their own functional applications directly in the browser.

sections.heading: What Happened
paragraphs: Building applications was once strictly the domain of skilled software developers. Now, start-ups are emerging that use AI to guide novices through the app creation process. One such service is Kineto, a spin-out from popular developer tool company JetBrains.

The Kineto platform operates entirely within a web browser, eliminating the need to download software or install complex environments. Users simply describe the app they want, select a color scheme, and an AI-driven system generates the first version.

Behind the scenes, multiple AI agents collaborate on tasks ranging from writing code to generating images and conducting tests. According to Kineto founder Andrew Zakonov, the platform utilizes various large language models to handle different aspects of the development process.

sections.heading: Why It Matters
paragraphs: This shift democratizes software creation, potentially turning any user into an app developer. Currently, creating a functional app through Kineto takes about an hour or two and requires roughly 10 iterations of testing and refinement. However, Zakonov anticipates this time dropping to just 30 minutes in the near future.

The platform is particularly targeting online content creators, such as YouTubers, who might want to offer interactive experiences like courses to their audiences. Kineto offers premium tiers with additional storage and AI credits to support these professional use cases.

sections.heading: The Bigger Picture
paragraphs: Despite the accessibility of these new tools, traditional app developers are not entirely convinced of their market disruption. Jules Goldberg, founder of Reviva Softworks and creator of the SnoreLab app (which boasts 15 million downloads), acknowledges the thrill of accessible creation but doubts AI-generated apps will become mainstream.

Goldberg argues that humans inherently prefer the convenience of downloading polished, existing software over building their own. He also points out that the ease of creating simple apps, like habit trackers, effectively devalues them in the marketplace.

"Even if it becomes easier to build a superficial app, it's enormously expensive to get that app in front of people," Goldberg notes, highlighting the enduring market advantage of established applications.

sections.heading: What's Next
paragraphs: While complex applications still require significant bug fixing and specialized prompts to build correctly, the technology is advancing rapidly. Zakonov believes interactive apps represent the "next mainstream medium" for content delivery.

As these AI tools evolve, the barrier to entry for software creation will continue to lower. The industry is watching closely to see if this technology will primarily serve as a creative outlet for hobbyists or if it will fundamentally alter how commercial software is produced and distributed.
